entry.title: SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues
entry.content:
Are you interested in activating Joule and integrating it seamlessly into your system—but running into errors along the way? In my role as solution architect for our SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group I’ve worked with some of our first Joule customers and would like to share how you can avoid and resolve errors during the activation.

This blog highlights the most common SAP Joule issues encountered during provisioning, booster execution, and system integration.

You’ll also find clear, step-by-step guidance to help you quickly troubleshoot and succeed with your activation.
Please also refer to our SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule Health Check blog to test your activation and configuration is working correctly before using Joule https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-and-joule-health-checks/ba-p/14348321

We will look at the following topics:

  1. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) subaccount / provisioning issues
  2. Joule booster problems
  3. User Experience (UX) / icon appearance issues
  4. SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning service (IPS) and Identity Authentication service (IAS) issues
  5. Restrictions/ constraints
  6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
  7. References
  8. How to contact SAP Support

1. SAP BTP subaccount / provisioning issues

Issue

Solution/Available fix

You have a license for an SAP product that supports Joule integration (such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, etc.) and after creating a subaccount the Joule service is not available in the subaccount entitlement.

Check SAP Help:Data Centers Supported by Joule

The data centre is supported but the entitlements are not available in your global account.

Contact your SAP Account Manager/Executive. 

Refer to SAP Note 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Help:Joule prerequisites

The menu entry "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of your SAP BTP Cockpit.

You are not an administrator of the Global account.

SAP Note 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of SAP BTP Cockpit

SAP Note 2669325 - How to add a user as a SAP Business Technology Platform Global Account Administrator

SAP systems are not visible in SAP BTP under the system landscape.

 

 

Before adding your system, make sure it's not already auto-discovered and listed automatically on the System Landscape ---> Systems page.

Refer to SAP Help: BTP Registering SAP System
SAP Note 3661817 - SAP systems are missing in System Landscape of the BTP Cockpit

 2. Joule Activation/Issues during the execution of the Joule booster

While running the booster configuration for the Joule Service, the prerequisites check failed for the Identity Authentication Tenant.

 An error message is displayed: "Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant."

 

Ensure that the IAS tenant is visible under the global account and configure trust between the Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and the Joule subaccount. 

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

 

 

 

The Joule booster is being run, and you encounter the following error:

*********************************
Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host <IAS tenant>. 
Report an incident in the BC-CP-MP component 

The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant isn't connected to the correct SAP BTP Global Account, or it's linked to the wrong customer ID.
How to Fix it:
a. Make sure your IAS tenant is linked to the correct Global Account.
Check the SAP Help:View Assigned Tenants and Admins.

b. If you have multiple IAS tenants , use the IAS tenant that matches the Global Account's customer ID.
Important:Data syncing between systems usually takes up to one week.Wait for this to complete before running the booster again so that the SAP IAS system appears in all the accounts.

You can manually trigger the sync via the “Refresh” button.
SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

Enabling Joule leads to the following error: 
"Prerequisites for the formation type "Integration with Joule" are not fulfilled.
No SAP Cloud identity service system is showing up under“System landscape” --->System

 

A new SAP BTP Global Account will take some time to synchronize all existing customer systems, but you can manually trigger the sync via the "Refresh" button.
This should ensure that your SAP Cloud Identity Services system shows up. Afterwards you can proceed with the Joule configuration.

 

The SAP Build Work Zone system does not appear in the dropdown list during the setup process (Joule Booster - Step 5 Set Up Integrations).

 

 

The SAP Build Work Zone service was not subscribed to under the relevant subaccount.
An instance of this service is not enough for the integration; therefore, the system will not appear under the options.

Add the SAP Build Work Zone subscription in the Joule subaccount. Refer to
SAP Note 3710864 - SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

Unable to find the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition option in Step 3 - "Select Integration" stage dropdown during activation by means of the Joule Booster.

In global account Systems, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system is already auto-discovered.

Ensure that it is visible in Joule Booster Step 04  "Select Capabilities", select "S/4HANA Private Cloud" and proceed to complete the Joule booster.

Refer to SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster for detailed information.

Error message: "The subscription of subaccount <xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxx> to application das-application and plan foundation, failed with state: SUBSCRIBE_FAILED."

Observe the error messages indicating SaaS Subscription failed and instructions to open a support case or unsubscribe and retry.

 

Refer to SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster for detailed instructions.

If the issue persists, raise a support incident and choose component CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB for further investigation.

 

 

 3. UX/ Icon appearance issue

Joule refused to frame 'https://xxx.xxx.cloud.sap/' because an ancestor violates the following Content Security Policy directive: "frame-ancestors”.                             

 

 

 

 

Enable 3rd party cookies of your browser.Add the trusted domain in SAP IAS and SAP BTP
a. Add trusted domain in SAP IAS in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
Go to Applications & Resources -->Tenant Settings --> Trusted Domains--> Click the + (Add) button
Type the URL of the trusted host into the field.

b. Add the trusted domain in SAP BTP.
In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your specific subaccount ---> Security ---> Settings.
Click the + (Add) button.
Enter the required hostname, for example: https://<your-s4-or-sfsf-url>.com

The Joule icon at the top app bar in the Fiori Launchpad is not properly displayed.

The issue is caused using an unsupported SAPUI5 version in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. 

 

Upgrade the SAPUI5 Component version of SAP S/4HANA to be equal to or higher than the minimum supported version.

SAP Notes:
2346367 - How to upgrade the SAPUI5 library in ABAP - SAP for Me
3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
 

After integrating Joule with SAP Solutions, the user is unable to launch Joule by clicking the diamond icon,  it fails to load or respond.

 

 

Allow third-party cookies.

SAP Note 3643889 - User unable to launch the Joule Icon after integrating with SAP Solutions

The Joule icon is not appearing for a specific user despite that the correct role-based permissions have been assigned in SAP SuccessFactors.

The issue is most likely caused by security policies of the network provider, which may be blocking certain URLs that are required for the icon to appear.
The affected user does not have the General User Permission --->Access to Joule

Refer to SAP Note 3685621 - Joule button not appearing on SuccessFactors

After clicking on the Joule icon in SAP SuccessFactors, it tries to load for a few minutes and subsequently the user gets a blank or greyed-out screen.

Refer to SAP Note 3544482 - Getting Error / White /Blank / IAS login Screen When Trying to Launch Joule in SuccessFactors

4. SAP IPS/IAS issues

The SAP IAS source system read job is failing.

 

Keep the source system name the same as in the content channel that had been created in the standard SAP Build Work Zone.

Joule was successfully integrated with the SAP product. However, clicking the Joule icon results in a blank white or SAP IAS login screen. Single sign-on is not working.

 

Check the SAP IAS domain. In the SAP BTP Joule subaccount, go to Trust Configuration and select the Custom Identity Provider for Applications.

Change the field "Domain" to the one used in the SAP product.

Refer to SAP Note 3635949 - Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

5. Restrictions /Constraints
SAP Joule operates within specific constraints.
SAP notes:
3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
3711505 - Restrictions: Multiple different Work Zone editions integration with Joule
Before starting the Joule project, please refer to the document below. All constraints applicable to Joule are listed here.
SAP Help:Joule constraints

6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
You may experience issues that would have to be reported in SAP incidents. In such cases, please collect information, which will help the support team to investigate and resolve the problem quickly.
SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Jouletroubleshooting
SAP Help:Monitoring and Troubleshooting

7. How to report issues
You can report an incident or error through SAP for Me. Ensure that you provide a detailed description of the issue including the steps to reproduce it. Attach any relevant screenshots, logs, or error messages to help with the investigation. Please use the appropriate components listed below when creating your incident.

General support component for core Joule services issue and functionality

CA_JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

SaaS Subscription error after running Joule Booster

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

8. Frequently asked questions

  1. Are there options available to customize the theme of Joule so it aligns with our branding guidelines and specific colour palette?

The theme tab is only available when Joule is used in a standalone (single page) mode. For now, Joule automatically adapts to the theme of the embedding application.  For example, if the user will changes the theme in the SAP Work Zone settings, Joule will automatically adopt it. The user cannot choose a different theme for the Joule client.
SAP Note 3646989 - Is it possible to adjust theming for Joule?

  1. Is it possible to remove the Joule icon for selected users?

When Joule is enabled through the Joule booster, it is activated for all users in both SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems. At present, there is no supported option to restrict or disable Joule for specific users or user groups.
SAP Note 3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Summary
In this blog, we reviewed common configuration and activation issues related to Joule including challenges with SAP Business Technology Platform provisioning, Joule booster activation, UX visibility, and integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services (IPS/IAS), along with key restrictions, monitoring tips, and reference resources.

In addition, we discussed current system restrictions and limitations, monitoring options, log analysis techniques, and practical troubleshooting steps to help identify and resolve issues efficiently.

By following the guidance and best practices outlined above, administrators can streamline Joule activation, minimize configuration errors, and ensure a smoother user experience. If problems persist, contact SAP Support with detailed information such as error messages, screenshots, tenant details, and relevant logs to accelerate root cause analysis and resolution.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: Re: How to show custom field from app: supplier invoice t...
entry.content:
Dear @Prasit_Arpornrat,

SAP does support adding a custom field to the supplier invoice header in the supplier invoice creation process. For Create Supplier Invoice, SAP documents app extensibility for the business context Procurement: Supplier Invoice Header and the custom field can be enabled for the header UI data source SI_HEADER_GUI. However, Manage Supplier Line Items is based on a different extensibility object. SAP explicitly states in the FAQ for this app that to display a custom field there the field must be created in business context Accounting: Journal Entry Item and then enabled for the Manage Supplier Line Items app. SAP app extensibility page for the app confirms the same business context, because of that a field created only in the supplier invoice header context is not documented by SAP as automatically flowing through and becoming visible in Manage Supplier Line Items after posting

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/a7c98ab55d8d47ccbfb12303b0e60c35.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/d65648a82f914d90bcdb32008010b291.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/aa935490094b441e9444ed74afba4d41.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/656fa2cdaaa9480b83d8bda9e9a57fe1.html

If the business requirement is to see that value after posting in Manage Supplier Line Items the supported design is to create or use a custom field in Accounting: Journal Entry Item and populate it during the posting process. SAP documents Process Extensibility for Journal Entries for Supplier Invoice (Business Transaction 0300) under Accounting: Journal Entry Item and SAP also documents substitution support for journal entry items and extension fields

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/21f32a09ebf34882abbda7fecb758241.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/950f8054747b053de10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/6d95f6fa596646f59cfb9dd0dd7b7fd3.html

Summary:

  1. Create the field on Procurement: Supplier Invoice Header if the business wants to capture it during invoice entry
  2. Create the corresponding field on Accounting: Journal Entry Item for reporting/display in Manage Supplier Line Items
  3. Populate the journal entry item field during posting using the supported finance extensibility approach for Supplier Invoice (Business Transaction 0300)

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to Configure Replenishment Jobs (SAP Commerce B2B)
entry.content:
Hi all,

I’m adding more details to my original question because I’ve identified the issue more clearly.

I am able to create a replenishment order from the frontend, and the system correctly generates a CronJob (B2BAcceleratorCartToOrderJob).

However, when I manually trigger the job, it fails with the following error:

No process definition known by name 'replenishmentOrderProcess'

From my analysis:

  • The file replenishmentOrderProcess.xml exists inb2bacceleratoraddon (deprecreted extension)
  • It is correctly defined as a Spring bean (ProcessDefinitionResource)
    <bean id="replenishmentOrderProcessDefinitionResource" class="de.hybris.platform.processengine.definition.ProcessDefinitionResource" >
        <property name="resource" value="classpath:/b2bacceleratoraddon/processes/replenishmentOrderProcess.xml"/>
	</bean>

So it seems that the process definition is not being loaded/registered by the process engine at runtime.

My questions are:

  • Is there any additional configuration required to register replenishmentOrderProcess in the processDefinitions list?


Any guidance or confirmation on the correct setup would be really helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Query on Pack Instructions functionality to do package building in Freight Unit.
entry.content: We are using Pack Instructions functionality to do package building in Freight Unit. Now Product master has weight and volume of say 10 LB and 10 Cubic Feet and it is packed in 11 LB (total weight including tare weight) and 11 Cubic Feet total volume closed box. What happens is we have variant configuration and the sales order line has for the same product master - at line-item level weight 15 LB and 15 Cubic Feet. So it appears in FU package hierarchy that 11 LB, 11 Cubic Feet box is holding 15 LB, 15 Cubic Feet cargo. Is it correct that pack instruction only looks at pack instruction master data and not at sales order line-item data?

entry.title: Asset Viewer and Asset Overview app gaps
entry.content:
2 questions related to 2 critical apps for EAM

Asset Viewer app

why is navigation window so super small at the left and is so much space blank at the right. from a UX perspective this is far from useless for endusers. hope its a quick win to improve by expanding the window at the left at least 

Asset Overview app

this new 2602 was highly anticipated within our organisation to remove some of the gaps so far between Public Cloud and our original ECC setup. several asks 

  • Why not possible to expand to full view technical object, equipment, BOM, material. See Asset viewer technical object as reference.
  • why so limited amount of columns/slicers available 
  • link to measurement points history across the technical object or at individual equipment levels in the roadmap? 
  • Cumulative risk visual in the roadmap? 

keen to hear how other companies cope with this and to hear from SAP when/what will be changed in these 2 apps. Are last 2 bullets covered by different SAP products like APM or anticipated to have this also within Public Cloud EAM as basic functionality?

entry.title: Re: Yes, SAP, actually language independent texts in a text view is supported.
entry.content:
Hello @CLTGravesen 

very interesting what you found out, and how you solved it 😉

You are right, there are still a few gaps in things "which worked for 20 years" in BW, and still don't ... but this is true for "all" new SAP systems ... another example in S4 Public Cloud ... it took more than 4 years now, that you can launch an App by it's F-number, e.g. /nF1733 ... it seems a working student could develop this in 2 weeks... but actually it seems rocket sience.

Another solution idea to your problem:

You could also have created one general table with all relevant language keys for your system: DE, EN, FR etc. whatever you need, and then use a Cross join, to multiply the language-independent texts with your language keys and have a view, which you can associate e.g. in your fact view? Would this not work as well?

Have a nice weekend ... and I'm ooking forward to the next "easter egg" blog 😜

BR, Martin

entry.title: How to remove/delete form from SAP Build Process Automation Inbox after due date?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working with SAP Build Process Automation and have a requirement related to task/forms in the Inbox.

Once a task reaches its due date, I want to automatically remove or delete the associated form/task from the SAP Build Inbox.

Currently, even after the due date is passed, the form still remains visible in the Inbox.

Could you please help with the following:

  • Is there a way to automatically delete or archive forms after the due date?

  • Can this be handled using workflow configuration, timers, or any automation?

  • Are there any best practices to manage expired tasks in the Inbox?

I have already explored workflow settings but could not find an option for automatic removal.

Any guidance or recommended approach would be helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Updated Transport to Production Strategy for Rapid Activation
entry.content:
Hello i have a question about the SAP Fiori Rapid Activation
Transport Move to Production Strategy How-To-Guide, particularly about the execution in subsequent systems. Until version 1.4 you can see separate tasks with CTS are not checked. In the last Versionens for S4HANA 2023 and 2025 you can see for Tasklists SAP_GW_FIORI_ERP_ONE_CLNT_SETUP and SAP_FIORI_FOUNDTION_S4 for execution in subsequent systems specially Tasks CTS Customizing and Workbench are marked for execution. 

 

This is in QA/Production not possible. 

The Settings für execution tasklist like SAP _FIORI_FCM_CONTEN_ACTIVATION you see the Task CTS is not checked. 

 

Has somebody find a correct Document Version for SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA Rapid Activation Transport Move to Production Strategy How-To-Guide? Or you think the settings für SAP_GW_FIORI_ERP_ONE_CLNT_SETUP and SAP_FIORI_FOUNDTION_S4 in subsequent systems for CTS are right? 

I welcome any feedback.

Edaurd Dauenhauer

entry.title: Title: From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This March
entry.content:
In the age of AI, the skills we build today will define how we work tomorrow. That’s why we’ve launched a monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

Building on my first blog, where I introduced you to our AI live sessions Beyond Hype (which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI), I wanted to address some of your most asked questions. But before I do that, I’m excited to share that the sessions are now ungated and no longer require a SAP Learning Hub subscription!

  1. What methodology should customers follow to modernize with RISE with SAP, and what environment prerequisites must customers meet to enable the AI toolchain?

    The recommended approach for modernization is the RISE with SAP methodology—a single, standardized framework that guides customers end-to-end across their transformation journey. Built on SAP Activate and enriched with proven best practices, it provides a structured path from discovery to run, while embedding clean core principles and quality gates to ensure consistency, governance, and partner alignment throughout. 

    This methodology is complemented by an integrated toolchain enabled by SAP Business AI. With the RISE with SAP package, key tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM are already included, so that customers can start without additional licenses for the standard AI-powered setup. However, the bundled offering covers foundational capabilities. Advanced or specialized features in Signavio and LeanIX may require additional licenses.

    Beyond the commercial bundling, SAP also supports customers operationally through onboarding and the Transformation Preparation Service (TPS), helping set up and configure the toolchain within the customer’s landscape from day one.



  2. How are changes in solutions like SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM automatically synchronized across the toolchain?

    SAP Signavio is the leading system for process modelling; it’s where processes are designed, planned, and maintained. These models are then pushed to SAP Cloud ALM, where they are used to run and manage projects. If changes to a process are required, they are made in SAP Signavio first, and the updated models are then re-synchronized with SAP Cloud ALM. This ensures a clear “single source of truth” in Signavio, with Cloud ALM always working on the latest version.

  3. What do I need to get started with Joule agents — from technical prerequisites to activation steps?

    Getting started with Joule agents is straightforward once you understand the discovery and provisioning process. Here's a high-level walkthrough:

    Step 1: Discover Available Agents
    Start by exploring the AI Catalog in SAP Discovery Center. As explained in the first question, there you'll find the complete list of available agents, along with details about their capabilities, availability status (GA, Early Adopter Care, or Beta), and pricing.

    Step 2: Evaluate and Trial
    If you find an agent that fits your needs, check whether it's available for trial. The Discovery Center provides all the necessary information, including technical prerequisites such as BTP subaccount requirements, entitlements, connectivity settings, and configuration details.

    Step 3: Provision the Agent
    Once your organization has purchased the required entitlements, you can provision the agent directly through SAP for Me. We're also introducing enhanced capabilities that will guide you through the provisioning process, including the selection of prerequisite landscapes. BTP will handle the necessary connections in the background, as these are already configured within your BTP landscape- making activation smooth and seamless.

    What if I am still on-premise? Joule agents are cloud-native and run on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). On-premise customers can access these agents by connecting their systems to BTP via integration solutions such as SAP Integration Suite or BTP connectivity services. This allows them to leverage the power of AI agents while keeping their core business systems on-premise.

    Read on to get answers to your top questions from the session in this blog.

In this three-part tutorial series, you can rewatch how Joule Agents embedded in core business processes such as CX, HR, and Finance help organizations move from insight to action. You’ll learn where agents can proactively support decisions, execute process steps, and reduce manual effort.

Human Resource Agent: Shift into a manager's perspective. In this demo, we’ll focus on how managers not only support their teams day to day but also track and evaluate performance over time. A key part of this is the regular review cycles, whether quarterly, mid-year, or annual, where managers assess progress, provide feedback, and guide their teams toward better outcomes. You’ll see how this process can be streamlined and enhanced with intelligent support.

 

Customer Experience Agent: Step into the role of a sales representative or sales manager within the Sales Cloud application. You’ll see how users can easily access their assigned leads and opportunities, and more importantly, how they can take the next best actions supported by intelligent sales and service agents. The focus is on how AI-driven insights help prioritize work, guide decision-making, and ultimately drive more efficient and effective sales execution.

 

Finance Agent: Let’s put ourselves in the shoes of an accountant. In this demo, we’ll focus on one of the more time-consuming aspects of finance, managing accruals during month-end, quarter-end, or year-end cycles. While finance should enable strategic insights, much of the effort often goes into repetitive administrative tasks like journal entries and estimations. You’ll see how an accounting accrual agent can take on this workload, helping automate entries, improve accuracy, and free up time for more value-driven activities.

 

Want to dive deeper into the content? Rewatch the session and access the session material:

  • Accelerate your transformation to the Cloud with SAP Business AI (recording) 
  • Efficient Document Inbound Processing in SAP Cloud ERP with SAP Document AI (recording)

Hi Joule, where should I get started with the latest learning resources? *

Apart from live enablement sessions, explore our latest on-demand learning resources:

  • Introducing Joule Learning Journey: Understand Joule and the business value it provides, its core components, role‑based context‑aware workflows, and adoption essentials.

* Powered by the machine learning technology of SAP AI Business Services, personalized recommendations are generated for employees, in one place on the Learning home page, based on employee data.

As we look ahead, we’re excited to share with you a new lineup of learning opportunities designed to help you expand your knowledge and make AI an advantage in your role:

  • April 8, 15:00 CET: Agent Assisted Coding with AI Core. Explore how to leverage SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub to enable agent-assisted coding workflows in your enterprise development environment.
  • April 9, 10 am CET: Build AI securely -Avoid Pitfalls in the Development and Operations Lifecycle. Learn more about common security pitfalls across development and operations, from secret leaks in version control to missing checks and misconfigured infrastructure that expose credentials.
  • April 14, 3 pm CET: Activating and Monitoring SAP Business AI in SAP for Me. Learn how to activate SAP Business AI capabilities and monitor AI consumption using SAP for Me.

Stay tuned for more, and happy learning! 🚀

Further Resources:

entry.title: Using IAS to Secure Python APIs on Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
In this blog post, I'll demonstrate how to use SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication Service (IAS) to implement authentication for a Python API. This approach is particularly useful when building AI Agents or MCP servers that need protection from unauthorized access.

Scenario

Given an HTTP-based API, we want to implement an authentication check. This might be, for example, a backend service consumed by another service. In this case, the OAuth Client Credentials flow is the industry standard to secure that access. This flow deals with clients that have access to certain APIs within specific scopes.

For my use case, I'm currently working on securing an MCP server (detailed blog posts will follow on that topic). The expected result is that my API sits behind an authentication check, where only clients with proper client credentials and granular permissions can access it. IAS serves as the authorization authority and provides the infrastructure and UI to handle the creation of client credentials and scoping.

Prerequisites

To follow along with this blog post, you will need:

  • Proper privileges to deploy an app to Cloud Foundry
  • Admin access to an IAS tenant (you can always create one with the additional tenant plan on BTP or reuse an existing one)

Creating an Application in IAS

The first step to enable such a setup is to create an application in IAS. This can be done by logging in to the admin panel of your IAS tenant.

Once logged in, use the Create button to start the application creation process.

You'll have several options to choose from. Select SAP BTP Solution as the type (there are also some other special types available). For the protocol, choose OIDC and you don't need to select any parent application.

Once the application is created, we can specify the Provided APIs - these work similar to scopes. Here we can define different APIs we want to expose. For example, I create:
- One scope for Read Access: `api_read_access`
- One scope for Write Access: `api_write_access`

Now we can go ahead and create individual access keys with selected APIs. Let's start with a full access key. To do this, create a new secret in the Secrets section of the Client Authentication menu of your application. Select the API Access level - in which I only opt for Application and OIDC - and then select the API Permission Groups. Here we see the different provided APIs from earlier. For full access, I select both APIs.

Later on, for demonstration purposes, I also create one key with only the read access permission.

Once a secret is created you will be able to copy its value and store it in a secure place.

This completes the setup on the IAS side. To summarize: We have an application with a Client ID and have created several scoped secrets. Now we can go ahead and implement the authentication check on the Python side.

Implementing the Authentication Check

Here's the Python implementation using FastAPI:

import os
import requests
import jwt
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse

# Config
ISSUER = "https://ias-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com"
JWKS_URL = f"{ISSUER}/oauth2/certs"
AUDIENCE = "1d3656f2-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx88b43b7"


def get_public_key(token: str):
    """Holt JWKS und findet den passenden Public Key"""
    kid = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)["kid"]
    jwks = requests.get(JWKS_URL).json()

    for key in jwks["keys"]:
        if key["kid"] == kid:
            return jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(key)

    raise Exception("No matching key found")


def verify_token(token: str):
    """Validiert JWT Token"""
    public_key = get_public_key(token)
    payload = jwt.decode(token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"],
                        audience=AUDIENCE, issuer=ISSUER)

    if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
        raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

    return payload

# FastAPI App
app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def auth_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

    if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
        return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": "Missing token"})

    token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

    try:
        payload = verify_token(token)
        request.state.user = payload
    except Exception as e:
        return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": str(e)})

    return await call_next(request)

@app.get("/secure")
def secure(request: Request):
    return {
        "message": "authorized"
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", 8080)))

Let's examine the code sample in detail:

We start with a basic FastAPI server that has a `/secure` endpoint that needs to be protected. This is accomplished by adding a middleware that checks the Authorization header of every request. Specifically, we expect a Bearer token, which is then verified.

The verification process is particularly interesting. First, we need to retrieve the public keys from the IAS tenant we're using. JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) is a set of keys containing the public keys used to verify JSON Web Tokens (JWT) issued by the authorization server.

We use the PyJWT library to decode the token and verify its signature with the public key. This is a cryptographic process that ensures the token is indeed valid and was issued by IAS. Once the content is verified, we can implement the specific checks we need.

In this example, we check for the scope - specifically whether the service key actually has the `api_read_access` permission group.

On the backend side, we specify the issuer and the audience for our authentication check:

- The issuer is the token issuer we want to trust
- The audience refers to the application (its client ID)

These two variables are hard-coded for this example, but in practice, you would want to pass them via an environment variable or a user-provided service to your app instance to ensure the code is independent of the current stage.


Deploying to Cloud Foundry

To make this example deployable on Cloud Foundry, we add a manifest like this:

---
applications:
- name: btp-ias-python
  memory: 1024M
  disk_quota: 4024M
  buildpack: python_buildpack
  command: python btp_ias_auth_example.py

With this manifest in place, we can run `cf push` and see the result in action.

Testing the Secured API

Now let's look at how to make requests from the client side:

# 1. Get Token
curl -X POST "https://ias-tenant.accounts.cloud.sap/oauth2/token" \
  -u "1d3656f2-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-7c69088b43b7:s?_Hexxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxc=1_" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials"

Result:

200: {"access_token":"eyJqa3UiOiJodHRwczovL2FzcW8wb2F6Mi5hY2NvdW5mZGFlOWM3......


# 2. Request with Token
curl -X GET "https://api-url.eu12.cfapps.ondemand.com/secure" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer TOKEN_HERE"

Result:

200: {'message': 'authorized'}

# 3. Request without Token (expect 401)
curl -X GET "https://api-url.eu12.cfapps.ondemand.com/secure"

Result:

401: {'detail': 'Missing token'}

On the client side, we follow the standard OAuth2 token retrieval flow: we provide our client ID and client secret (from one of the keys we created earlier) to get an access token. Then we can pass this token in the Authorization header and make the request.

And voilà: we get the expected response!

Conclusion

In this blog post, we've seen how to:
1. Configure an application in IAS with scoped API permissions
2. Create client credentials with different permission levels
3. Implement JWT token verification in a Python FastAPI application
4. Deploy the secured API to Cloud Foundry
5. Test the authentication flow with different scenarios

This pattern provides a robust and scalable way to secure your Python APIs using SAP's Identity Authentication Service, with fine-grained control over which clients can access which endpoints.

I hope you enjoyed this blog post and found it helpful for your own projects!

entry.title: SAP Signavio - naming convention for tasks in German produces warning when modelling English process
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
We are using SAP Signavio and would now like to model processes in English in addition to our already existing German processes. For the German processes we have set up a convention check that checks the modeled process against the modeling guidelines. Among other things, the convention for naming tasks is "Object+Infinitive," which fits the German processes (e.g., "Material anlegen" [create material]) but not the English processes (e.g., "Create material"). As a result, in the English processes, the convention check now produces naming warnings when saving the processes ("Activity is named in the wrong style (Expected: Object+Infinitive; found: Verb+Object)").

Do you have any idea what can be done about this? Is there a possibility to maintain the convention check per language, or should this actually work automatically and is a bug?

Many thanks!!

entry.title: Re: Workflow for Park &app; Approve before Payment Posting
entry.content:
Dear @SunilKumar97,

In Public Cloud there is no standard workflow to park a normal manual outgoing payment entry and then approve/reject it before posting. The standard Post Outgoing Payments app posts and clears a single outgoing payment in one step

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/7e968055c4ed8065e10000000a44147b.html

However, SAP does support maker-checker controls for normal outgoing payments at the payment run / payment batch / bank approval stage. In standard Manage Automatic Payments is used to schedule payment proposals or payments, Revise Payment Proposals allows users to review and revise the proposed payments and Approve Bank Payments allows users to approve or reject individual payments or entire payment batches. SAP current Public Cloud feature scope also states that outgoing payments can be approved or rejected before the payment files are sent to banks

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/45698054f87c033de10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/de567c542889063de10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/c401ac923da649359cb8052438b0d9be.html

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

Summary:

  • No for a standard workflow on the manual outgoing payment posting entry itself
  • Yes for maker-checker approval/rejection of normal outgoing payments in the payment batch / bank payment approval process before bank transmission

Additional information: SAP has a separate delivered workflow for supplier down payment requests but as you noted, your question is about normal payments, not down payment requests

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/c13ec2e1616540459e1e9dbb2bf9c8ad.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: How to improve warehouse management processes with SAP Signavio
entry.content:
As Warehouse Process Owners and Supply Chain Transformation Leaders,  are you looking to gain deeper visibility into your warehouse operations, reduce inefficiencies, and accelerate your supply chain transformation?
SAP Signavio, teamed up with Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), offers a powerful combination of process intelligence and execution capability. Below is a curated set of resources to help you get started — from a quick product overview to hands-on demos and a forward-looking roadmap.
---

 

1. Get the Big Picture: SAP Signavio for EWM Overview
[Watch the Signavio EWM Overview Clip (3min)]

 

Not sure how SAP Signavio for EWM fits in your supply chain operating model? Start here. This short clip gives a crisp introduction to SAP Signavio for Extended Warehouse Management — what it does, where it fits, and why it matters for modern warehouse operations. A perfect first watch before diving into the deeper resources below.
---

 

2. See It in Action: Signavio + EWM Webcast
**Meet the Expert: SAP Signavio Process Mining for EWM on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition**
[Watch the Webcast on March 24th 2026, or Recording (60min)]

 

This expert session (on-demand recording) walks you through how SAP Signavio Process Analytics and Mining solution connects directly to EWM on S/4HANA Cloud, private edition. You will see how process flows are automatically discovered, deviations are surfaced, and data-driven insights drive continuous improvement. Ideal for process architects and transformation leads who want to understand the *why, what and how* to protect and accelerate supply chain process transformations.
---

 

3. Hands-On: Click-Through Demo for Supply Chain Excellence
**Click-Demo: Supply Chain Excellence (EWM) with SAP Signavio**
[Explore the interactive Demo (10-15min)]

 

Ready to experience the end to end EWM transformation user journey in the Signavio Solution Suite yourself? This self-guided demo lets you experience the full SAP Signavio + EWM journey at your own pace — from performance baseline definition, via improvement opportunities to value realisation. No system access required. Share it with your team or use it to build the business case internally.
---

 

4. What's Coming: SAP Signavio Process Intelligence EWM Outbound Value Accelerator on the SAP Roadmap
**SAP Roadmap Explorer — new Embedded EWM Outbound Value Accelerator**
[View on SAP Roadmap Explorer]

 

Keep an eye on what's next. The upcoming **Embedded EWM Outbound Value Accelerator** for SAP Signavio Process Intelligence is planned for General Availability in May 2026 and promises to further simplify and accelerate time-to-value for outbound warehouse processes. Check the roadmap entry for the latest details and plan your adoption timeline accordingly.
---

 

5. Deepen Your Understanding: Background Reading & Learning
**Blog: Traditional Business KPI Approach vs. Business Process Monitoring**
[Read on SAP Community]

 

A thought-provoking read that challenges the way many organizations measure warehouse and supply chain performance. It explains why static KPI dashboards often miss the root cause of process issues — and how continuous business process monitoring delivers the actionable intelligence that KPIs alone cannot provide.
---

 

6. Ready for a real demo experience?
**Request a real solution demo from our Signavio solution experts**
[Click to request a demo]

 

Your Next Step
Whether you are just exploring what SAP Signavio can do for your warehouse operations, or you are already mid-transformation and looking for concrete tools to accelerate, the resources above give you a clear path forward:

 

1. **Watch** the EWM overview clip to set the context.
2. **Replay** the webcast to understand the overarching user-journey through the Signavio solution suite. 
3. **Click through** the demo to experience it first-hand.
4. **Check the roadmap** to align your planning with upcoming innovations.
5. **Read the blog** to reframe how your organization thinks about process performance.
6. **Ready** for a real demo experience?

 

Questions or want to explore further? Reach out — this space is evolving fast, and there has never been a better time to put process intelligence at the heart of your warehouse transformation.

 

---
### Additional Resources (For the Curious and the Committed)
 
Business Content Reference Guide — SAP Signavio Process Insights
Full reference for all pre-built Signavio Process Insights performance indicators.
Filter the first column *"Performance Indicator ID"* for entries containing "EWM" to see exactly what is available out of the box.
 
Mastering Process Harmonization with SAP Signavio — SAP Learning
A structured learning course covering SAP Signavio's Process Landscape Analysis capability — essential for organizations managing multiple sites or harmonizing processes across an ERP transformation. 
 
SAP Signavio Value Accelerators — Supply Chain Management (L2 Deck)
A concise document outlining how SAP Signavio Value Accelerators can fast-track your supply chain transformation.

entry.title: Governing the Emerging Collective Intelligence
entry.content:
Artificial intelligence is often described as a technological breakthrough. But what if it is something more fundamental? Today’s AI systems are trained on vast amounts of human-generated data, documents, transactions, conversations, and decisions. In doing so, they do not simply process information. They reorganise fragments of our shared intellectual and cultural history.

When AI systems retrieve and interpret this data, they surface not only knowledge, but also ambiguity, bias, contradiction, and unresolved human tension. What emerges is not purely artificial intelligence-it is a reflection of humanity itself. This raises a profound question: Are we building systems that replicate human reasoning, or systems that externalise our collective mind?

From Collective Unconscious to Data Lakes

Carl Jung introduced the idea of the collective unconscious—a deeper layer of shared psychological structures that shape how humans perceive, think, and act. Modern enterprises are, in many ways, building a technological equivalent. Data lakes, enterprise platforms, and AI systems aggregate vast amounts of collective experience—sales interactions, customer behaviour, operational decisions—into shared repositories. From these, patterns emerge. Insights form. Decisions are guided.

But there is a critical difference. The collective unconscious was invisible. Enterprise intelligence systems are not. They are queryable, operational, and increasingly autonomous. This shift transforms something deeply human into something actionable—and scalable.

The Mirror and the Shadow

If AI systems reflect our collective knowledge, they also reflect our limitations. They can amplify:

  • Biases embedded in historical data
  • Misaligned incentives within organisations
  • Incomplete or conflicting interpretations of reality

In this sense, AI is not just a tool. It is a mirror—one that shows both clarity and distortion. Without proper governance, this mirror does not simply reflect reality. It can reshape it.

From Intelligence to Responsibility

This is where the conversation must evolve. AI governance is often framed as a technical or regulatory concern—focused on compliance, accuracy, and control. These are necessary, but not sufficient. If AI represents an externalised form of collective intelligence, then governance becomes something more: AI governance is the stewardship of how human knowledge, decisions, and meaning are captured, interpreted, and amplified by machines.

This introduces new responsibilities for organisations:

  • Not only to manage data, but to understand what it represents
  • Not only to deploy AI, but to question what it reinforces
  • Not only to optimise outcomes, but to ensure alignment with human intent

The Enterprise Imperative

For enterprises, this shift is already happening. AI copilots, assistants, and agents are becoming embedded in daily workflows—supporting sellers, guiding decisions, and interpreting complex systems like compensation, forecasting, and customer engagement. These systems do more than automate tasks. They shape how people understand reality within the organisation. And that makes governance not optional—but foundational.

From SaaS to Governing Intelligence: The Role of Joule

Enterprise software is undergoing a fundamental transformation. For decades, SaaS systems were designed to store, process, and expose data. Their primary role was to provide structure, consistency, and transactional reliability across business processes. With the rise of AI, this role is expanding. Systems are no longer just systems of record or systems of engagement. They are becoming systems of interpretation—capable of explaining outcomes, guiding decisions, and interacting with users in natural language. This is where SAP Joule represents a significant shift. Joule is not simply an AI assistant embedded into enterprise applications. It acts as an interface to enterprise intelligence, connecting data, processes, and business context into a unified layer of understanding. But more importantly, Joule introduces the foundation for something new: A governed layer of enterprise intelligence.

As AI agents begin to interpret sensitive domains such as compensation, performance, customer interactions, and financial outcomes, the need for trusted interpretation becomes critical. This moves SAP’s role beyond delivering SaaS applications. It positions SAP as a provider of:

  • Context-aware intelligence, grounded in enterprise data
  • Traceable reasoning, connecting outputs back to underlying transactions
  • Governed AI interactions, aligned with business rules, policies, and intent

In this model, Joule is not only assisting users—it is operating within a controlled and explainable framework of enterprise knowledge. This is the transition:

  • From delivering applications
  • To enabling decisions
  • To govern how intelligence itself is produced and consumed within the enterprise

Why This Matters-As organisations adopt AI at scale, the risk is not only incorrect answers, but ungoverned meaning.

Without a governing layer, AI may generate plausible but ungrounded interpretations. Decisions may drift from policy and intent. Trust in enterprise systems may erode. With Joule and SAP’s AI foundation, there is an opportunity to ensure that:

  • Intelligence is grounded in enterprise truth
  • Outputs are explainable and auditable
  • AI becomes a trusted extension of the organisation, not an uncontrolled external layer

A Strategic Shift-This marks a broader transformation: From Software as a Service → to Governed Intelligence as a Capability

In this future, the value of enterprise platforms will not be defined only by the data they store—but by how responsibly they interpret and operationalise that data through AI.

A New Discipline

We are entering a new phase of AI adoption—one where the question is no longer what AI can do, but what it should represent. This requires a new discipline that connects:

  • Technology and psychology
  • Data and meaning
  • Automation and accountability

Because in the end, the systems we build will not only reflect our intelligence—They will reflect our values.

As enterprises continue to build and deploy AI, one question should remain at the centre: If AI becomes the voice of our collective intelligence—who ensures it speaks with clarity, responsibility, and truth?

entry.title: Re: Intercompany return process steps and scope item
entry.content:
Dear @winpavan90,

In Public Cloud BKP is the base scope item for the standard Customer Returns process. For the intercompany scenario, SAP refers to 2F4 as the additional scope item, so 2F4 should be understood as the intercompany extension of BKP, not as a completely separate returns proces

More details below:

The scope items can be found here: https://me.sap.com/processnavigator

 

Inside, you will find details about the process, such as the BPMN diagram and the test script which includes the detailed process flow and the configuration steps required to execute the process

 

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Service and asset manager MDK Versions
entry.content:
From the matrix, I would start with the SAM Metadata column and find the version you are using.  From there you can look at the combinations of MDK version and SAM MDK Plug in version.  So if you want to upgrade to a newer MDK version you MUST use the SSAM MDK Plug In associated with that version.  It can be a newer version than the SSAM metadata version.  For example you can build a client to run SSAM 2505 that uses MDK 25.9.1 with SAM MDK Plug In 2511.0.1.

Once you that that combination determined, I recommend you take the Branding SDK that most closely matches the SAM MDK Plug In version as the starting point for your SAM.mdkproject as they are settings, externals, config specific to those Plug In files in the .mdkproject needed to create the client correctly.

In the Software Download you can search MDK Client to find the download for the specific MDK version you are going to use from the matrix.

Hope this helps clarify it.

--Bill

entry.title: Re: How to style ALL FormCell header and caption in the S...

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Extending Joule with Custom Skills & AI Agents (Part 6 of 6 -Series Complete)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 6 OF 6  |  JOULE  |  SAP BUILD  |  AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: Extending Joule with Custom Skills and AI Agents

The final part of this series brings the AI pipelines from Parts 1–5 into Joule — building custom Joule skills backed by the CAP service from Part 5, assembling a multi-skill Joule agent for SAP knowledge Q&A and exception log triage, and covering prerequisites, BTP Destination configuration, Action project setup, Skill Builder, Agent Builder, and deployment.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 6 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Joule Studio (part of SAP Build) is where the AI pipelines from this series become conversational SAP capabilities accessible to any business user. A Joule Skill is a structured, rule-based capability that calls an API action. A Joule Agent goes further — it plans, reasons, and orchestrates multiple skills to handle complex multi-step queries. This article covers the complete path from BTP prerequisites through skill creation, agent assembly, and production deployment, connected directly to the CAP OData service built in Part 5.

Series Context and What This Part Covers

This is Part 6 — the final part — of the SAP Generative AI Hub series. Parts 1 through 5 built a complete AI stack: Generative AI Hub setup, Python SDK integration, prompt pipelines, HANA Cloud vector store with RAG, and a production CAP application on BTP Cloud Foundry.

This part surfaces all of that through Joule — SAP's AI copilot — so business users can interact with your AI capabilities in natural language without knowing anything about APIs, embeddings, or model calls. The CAP OData actions from Part 5 (searchMasterData and askKnowledgeBase) become Joule skills. Those skills are assembled into a Joule agent that handles multi-step SAP queries autonomously.

Joule Studio is a fast-evolving product. The Skill Builder reached General Availability in mid-2025. The Agent Builder became Generally Available in January 2026. Always verify current prerequisites and supported regions in the SAP Help Portal before beginning setup — regional availability and entitlement requirements have changed across releases.

ARCHITECTURE: HOW IT ALL CONNECTS 

End-to-End Architecture for This Series

Before building, it helps to see how Joule Studio connects to everything built in Parts 1–5:

User asks Joule → Joule Agent reasons →	Skill triggered → CAP OData action → AI Core + HANA → Grounded answer

Joule receives the user's natural language input and determines which skill or agent to trigger based on the description you provide during skill creation. The skill calls the CAP OData action deployed in Part 5. The CAP service calls AI Core and the HANA vector store. The grounded AI response travels back through CAP → Joule → user. The user sees a natural language answer. The entire AI stack is invisible to them.

 

Layer

Role

Joule Studio (SAP Build)

Conversational UI layer — skill and agent builder, deployment, testing

BTP Destination

Connection bridge from Joule Studio to the CAP service from Part 5

Action Project (SAP Build)

Defines which CAP OData endpoints Joule can call and how

Joule Skill

Structured capability — maps user intent to a specific Action call

Joule Agent

Autonomous orchestrator — plans and executes multiple skills for complex queries

CAP Service (Part 5)

Backend — AI Core calls, HANA vector retrieval, audit logging

STEP 1: PREREQUISITES AND BTP SETUP 

Prerequisites Before Opening Joule Studio

Joule Studio setup has more prerequisites than any other part of this series. Missing any of these will cause the deployment errors covered in the community Q&A section at the end of this article. Verify each item before proceeding.

Required BTP Entitlements

In your BTP Global Account, confirm the following entitlements are available and assigned to your subaccount:

Service

Plan

Note

SAP Build Process Automation

build-default (Application) plan

Required for Joule Studio access

Joule

foundation or standard (Application) plan

Required for Joule copilot runtime

SAP Cloud Identity Services

Identity Authentication + Provisioning

Must be activated and configured

SAP AI Core

Extended plan

Already in place from Part 1

HANA Cloud

hana plan

Already in place from Parts 4–5

Run the Joule Booster

The Joule Booster in BTP Cockpit is the recommended setup path. It configures the Joule formation, establishes trust between Joule and your integrated SAP systems, and sets up the required BTP Build Work Zone subscription. Without the Booster completing successfully, Joule Studio deployments will fail with Tenant Administration errors.

  1. Open your BTP Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule'
  2. Select the Joule (das-application) plan → choose your subaccount
  3. Select SAP Build Process Automation from the Capability Packages dropdown
  4. Review the Formation Name and select the correct Build Process Automation system instance
  5. Complete the Booster — confirm the success message before proceeding

If the Joule Booster fails partway through, do not retry immediately. Check BTP → System Landscape → Formations to see if a partial Joule formation was created. Delete it before re-running the Booster — orphaned formations are the most common cause of repeated Booster failures.

Activate Joule Studio Capability Package

After the Booster completes, activate the Joule Studio capability package within SAP Build Process Automation:

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Settings → Joule Studio
  2. Activate the Joule Studio capability package
  3. Confirm the Skill Builder and Agent Builder tiles appear in the SAP Build Lobby
STEP 2: BTP DESTINATION CONFIGURATION 

Configuring the BTP Destination for the CAP Service

Joule Studio connects to backend services exclusively through BTP Destinations. The CAP service deployed in Part 5 must be registered as a destination with specific additional properties that make it visible to SAP Build and Joule Studio.

Create the Destination in BTP Cockpit

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → your Joule subaccount → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

Name:                  AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
Type:                  HTTP
URL:                   https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
Authentication:        OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID:             <your CAP app client ID from BTP>
Client Secret:         <your CAP app client secret>
Token Service URL:     https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Required Additional Properties

These additional properties are mandatory — without them the destination is invisible to Joule Studio and SAP Build:

sap.processautomation.enabled   =  true
HTML5.DynamicDestination        =  true
WebIDEEnabled                   =  true

The sap.processautomation.enabled = true property is the most commonly missed configuration step. Destinations without this property do not appear in the Joule Studio destination browser, and there is no error message — the destination is simply absent from the list. Always verify this property is set before troubleshooting connectivity issues.

Register Destination in SAP Build Control Tower

After creating the BTP Destination, register it in SAP Build so Joule Studio can access it:

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Control Tower → Destinations
  2. Click Open in BTP Cockpit — confirm your destination appears in the list
  3. Return to Control Tower → Environments → Create or select your deployment environment
  4. In the environment, add the destination AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  5. Click Check Connection — confirm the green checkmark before proceeding
STEP 3: ACTION PROJECT — EXPOSING CAP ENDPOINTS TO JOULE 

Creating Action Projects for the CAP OData Actions

An Action Project in SAP Build defines which endpoints from your CAP service Joule can call, what inputs they accept, and what outputs they return. Actions are the bridge between destinations and Joule Skills. You create one Action Project per service, then select individual actions within each skill.

Create the Action Project

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Connectors → Actions → Create
  2. In Choose an API Source → select SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  3. Browse OData Destinations → select AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  4. Enter a name (e.g. AI Knowledge Service Actions) and description → Create

Configure the searchMasterData Action

From the list of available endpoints from your CAP service, select the searchMasterData action:

  1. Select POST searchMasterData from the endpoint list → Add
  2. On the Input tab — confirm query (String) and maxResults (Integer) are visible
  3. On the Output tab — confirm materialNumber, description, similarity, and aiSummary fields
  4. Use the Test tab to validate — enter a sample query and verify the response before saving
  5. Save → Release → Publish to Library

Configure the askKnowledgeBase Action

Repeat the process for the askKnowledgeBase action:

  1. Select POST askKnowledgeBase → Add
  2. Input tab — confirm question (String) and category (String, optional)
  3. Output tab — confirm answer, sources array, and modelUsed fields
  4. Test with a sample question — verify a grounded answer returns from your HANA vector store
  5. Save → Release → Publish to Library

Both actions must be Published to Library before they are visible in the Joule Skill Builder. Released but unpublished actions do not appear in the Browse All Actions dialog. If your actions are missing from the skill builder, check their publication status in Connectors → Actions.

STEP 4: BUILDING JOULE SKILLS 

Creating Custom Joule Skills in Skill Builder

Joule Skills are structured, deterministic capabilities — when Joule identifies a user intent that matches a skill's description, it triggers that skill and calls the configured action. Skills are ideal for focused, single-purpose tasks where the response structure is predictable.

The skill description is the most important field in the entire skill definition. Joule uses it to decide when to trigger this skill versus other skills or agents. A vague description produces inconsistent triggering. A precise description produces reliable triggering.

  ▶  Skill 1: SAP Knowledge Base Q&A 

Creating the askKnowledgeBase Skill

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Select Joule Skill tile → Next
  2. Name: AskSAPKnowledgeBase
  3. Description (critical — Joule uses this for intent matching):
Answers questions about SAP configuration, HANA procedures, migration runbooks, and internal SAP knowledge base documentation. Use when the user asks 'how do I', 'what is the process for', 'explain', or asks about SAP-specific procedures, error handling, or best practices.
  1. Review → Create → the Skill Builder canvas opens
  2. In the Trigger panel → disable 'Allow Joule to generate a response' (the action will provide the response)
  3. Configure Skill Inputs → Add Input: question (String, Required), category (String, Optional)
  4. Configure Skill Outputs → Add Output: answer (String), sources (Array), modelUsed (String)
  5. On the canvas, choose + → Call Action → Browse All Actions
  6. Select askKnowledgeBase from AI Knowledge Service Actions → Add
  7. Select Destination Variable → Create → name it AI_KNOWLEDGE_DEST → select AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE
  8. Map Inputs: question → question, category → category
  9. Map Outputs: answer → answer, sources → sources, modelUsed → modelUsed
  10. Add a Send Message step → configure the response card to display the answer and source documents
  11. Save → the skill is ready for testing

  ▶  Skill 2: Exception Log Triage 

Creating the Exception Log Triage Skill

This skill triggers the exception log triage pipeline from Part 3, exposed as a CAP OData action. Add an additional action to your Action Project for the exception log endpoint if not already present, then create the skill following the same pattern:

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Joule Skill
  2. Name: SAPExceptionLogTriage
  3. Description:
Analyzes SAP exception logs, error dumps, and system messages. Use when the user provides an error log, exception text, or asks to analyze, triage, or diagnose an SAP system error or runtime failure.
  1. Configure Inputs: logText (String, Required — the raw log content)
  2. Configure Outputs: severity (String), summary (String), actions (String)
  3. Add Call Action step → select triageExceptionLog from Action Project
  4. Map inputs and outputs → Add Send Message step with severity-coded response card
  5. Save

Joule Skill descriptions must be precise and distinct from each other. If two skills have overlapping descriptions, Joule will trigger unpredictably between them. Test with a range of natural language inputs in the debug interface before deploying to confirm each skill triggers reliably for its intended query type.

  STEP 5: BUILDING THE JOULE AGENT 

Assembling a Multi-Skill Joule Agent

A Joule Agent goes beyond individual skills. Where a skill handles one focused task deterministically, an agent plans and reasons — it understands a complex user query, decides which skills to invoke and in what order, handles the outputs, and synthesizes a final response. Agents are built in Joule Studio's Agent Builder, which became Generally Available in January 2026.

Principal propagation is enforced automatically — the user's identity in Joule passes through to every skill and CAP service call. Your agent runs within the user's security context, applying the same authorization checks as any other SAP system access.

Create the Agent

  1. Open SAP Build Lobby → Create → Select Joule Agent tile → Next
  2. Name: SAPAIAssistant
  3. Agent Instructions (natural language — define the agent's role and boundaries):
You are an SAP AI assistant with access to the organization's SAP knowledge base, master data, and system logs. Help users find SAP configuration guidance, search for relevant materials, and triage SAP exception logs. Always ground your answers in the retrieved context. If you cannot find relevant information, say so explicitly rather than generating a general answer. For exception logs, always provide severity, root cause, and recommended action.
  1. Select Model — choose from available foundation models in your AI Core instance (GPT-4o or Claude recommended for reasoning quality)
  2. Save the base agent configuration

Add Skills as Agent Tools

Skills are added to the agent as callable tools. The agent decides at runtime which tool to invoke based on the user's query and its instructions:

  1. In the Agent canvas → Tools section → Add Tool → Select Joule Skill
  2. Add AskSAPKnowledgeBase → the skill description appears as the tool description the agent uses for routing decisions
  3. Add SAPExceptionLogTriage → confirm the description is distinct enough from the knowledge base skill
  4. Optionally add searchMasterData as a third tool if master data search is a common use case

The agent uses each skill's description to decide which tool to call. The same description precision principle from skill creation applies here — if the skill descriptions are vague or overlapping, the agent's tool selection will be unreliable. Review all skill descriptions together before finalizing the agent.

Configure Human-in-the-Loop (Optional but Recommended)

Joule Studio agents support human-in-the-loop by default. For exception log triage — where the recommended action may affect production systems — configure the agent to request confirmation before presenting recommended actions:

  1. In Agent Settings → Human-in-the-Loop → Enable
  2. Configure confirmation prompts for high-severity triage outputs
  3. This ensures a human reviews critical recommendations before they are surfaced to end users
STEP 6: TESTING IN JOULE STUDIO 

Testing Skills and the Agent Before Deployment

Joule Studio provides a built-in testing and debug interface. Use it before deploying to production — it shows exactly which skill or tool the agent selected, what inputs were passed, what the action returned, and how the response was assembled.

Testing Individual Skills

  1. Open the skill → Choose Launch in the top right
  2. A standalone Joule test interface opens
  3. Enter test queries that should trigger this skill — verify the action is called and the response is correct
  4. Enter queries that should NOT trigger this skill — verify it does not activate
  5. Review the Design Console for any configuration errors

Testing the Agent

  1. Open the agent → Choose Test in the top right
  2. The agent debug interface opens — this shows the full execution log
  3. Test with these queries to validate routing across all skills:

 

'How do I handle integer overflow in a HANA delta load procedure?'

→ Expected: AskSAPKnowledgeBase triggered
'Analyze this SAP log: RABAX INCLUDE ZFI_HANDLER Line 847...'

→ Expected: SAPExceptionLogTriage triggered
'Find materials similar to hydraulic pump assembly'

→ Expected: searchMasterData triggered (if added as tool)
  1. Expand the execution log nodes — confirm tool selection, input mapping, action response, and final output at each step
  2. Adjust agent instructions or skill descriptions if routing is incorrect — re-test after each change

The execution log in the Agent test interface is the most valuable debugging tool in Joule Studio. When agent behavior is unexpected, the log shows exactly why — which tool was selected, what prompt the agent generated, and what the action returned. Always check the log before adjusting instructions or skill descriptions.

STEP 7: DEPLOYING TO PRODUCTION JOULE 

Deploying Skills and Agent to Production

Deploy Skills

  1. Open each skill → Choose Deploy
  2. Select the deployment environment configured in Control Tower
  3. Confirm the destination AI_SAP_KNOWLEDGE_SERVICE is selected for the destination variable
  4. Deploy → wait for status to show Deployed

Deploy the Agent

  1. Open the agent → Choose Deploy
  2. Select the same deployment environment
  3. Confirm all skills show as available tools in the deployment preview
  4. Deploy → the agent status changes to Active

Access via Joule Formation

Once deployed, your skills and agent are available through the Joule Formation — the configuration that connects Joule to your SAP landscape. Users access the custom capabilities through the same Joule interface they use in S/4HANA, Fiori, or SAP Build Work Zone. No additional UI development is required.

To verify deployment in the Joule Formation: BTP Cockpit → System Landscape → Formations → your Joule Formation → confirm the agent appears in the active capabilities list.

Skills and agents deployed in a development or test environment are not automatically available in the production Joule Formation. Each environment requires its own deployment. Confirm which environment is connected to your production Joule instance before deploying and testing with end users.

COMMON DEPLOYMENT ISSUES 

Issues You Will Encounter and How to Resolve Them

 

Issue

Root Cause

Resolution

Error 6011 — Tenant Administration Worker

Joule Booster did not complete successfully or a partial formation exists

Delete orphaned formations in System Landscape → re-run the Joule Booster completely

Destination not visible in Joule Studio

sap.processautomation.enabled = true missing from destination additional properties

Add the property in BTP Cockpit → Destinations → edit destination → Additional Properties

Action not visible in Skill Builder

Action was Released but not Published to Library

Open Action Project → Versions → Publish the released version to Library

Agent triggers wrong skill

Skill descriptions are overlapping or too generic

Rewrite skill descriptions to be distinct and intent-specific — test with boundary queries

404 on action call from skill

Destination variable in skill points to wrong environment or destination

Open skill → check destination variable mapping → re-deploy to correct environment

Principal propagation failing

Identity Authentication trust not configured between Joule subaccount and CAP service subaccount

Verify IAS trust configuration in both subaccounts — both must use the same IAS tenant

Series Complete — What You've Built

Across six articles, this series built a complete SAP Generative AI Hub stack from first API call to production Joule agent:

  • Part 1: BTP entitlements, AI Core service instance, resource group, first API call
  • Part 2: Python SDK across BAS notebooks, local scripts, and BTP applications — five SAP data use cases
  • Part 3: Prompt pipelines — sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning across four SAP workflows
  • Part 4: HANA Cloud vector store — REAL_VECTOR schema, SAP embedding model, RAG on SAP data
  • Part 5: CAP application on BTP Cloud Foundry — AI-powered OData APIs with HANA persistence and Integration Suite connectivity
  • Part 6: Joule Studio — custom skills and multi-skill agent backed by the entire AI stack

The architecture that connects all six parts is the same architecture that SAP's own AI Foundation runs on. The Generative AI Hub provides governed model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. CAP provides the application and API layer. Joule Studio provides the conversational interface. Each layer builds on the previous one — and each one is production-ready, auditable, and governed within the SAP BTP trust boundary.

If you followed this series end-to-end and have questions about any specific step — or if you've implemented a variation of this architecture in your own SAP landscape — share it in the comments. The patterns in this series are starting points. The community variations and extensions are where the most valuable knowledge lives.

Dear Experts, 

Good day.

I am currently configuring the Fiscal Year Variant in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using the standard variant SZ (Configurable Template Variant). I would like to maintain a year-dependent fiscal year variant.

Initially, I was able to successfully set up the SZ variant as year-dependent and define the custom fiscal year variant accordingly. However, during testing, I changed the setting to Calendar Year (by selecting the relevant checkbox).

After making this change, I am now unable to revert the configuration back to year-dependent. When attempting to do so, I encounter the following error message:

Actually, my intention was not to delete any SAP pre-delivered configuration. Also, the system currently does not contain any journal entries or postings. Based on my understanding, it should still be possible to adjust the fiscal year variant at this stage.

May I kindly seek further advice on whether there is any recommended approach to revert the configuration back to year-dependent?

Thank you very much in advance for your support.

Best regards, 

HY
